text: The CT scan shows a centrally located nodule. The surgeon begins by dissecting the inferior pulmonary ligament and performing a mediastinal lymphadenectomy, resecting levels nine and eight lymph nodes. A posterior view of the hilum reveals the resection of levels eight and seven lymph nodes. The anterior approach involves dissecting the superior pulmonary vein and identifying tributaries from the right upper lobe. The surgeon dissects the fissure, easily identifying the pulmonary artery and its branches to the median lobe on the right side. A bladder catheter guides the stapler to divide the horizontal fissure.
The surgeon faces the anterior part of the hilum, individually dissecting the veins of the anterior segment. The veins are then divided using a surgical stapler.

The surgeon carefully removes lymph nodes during dissection to isolate the arterial branch and segmentary bronchus. The arterial branch is visible in the foreground, with the segmentary bronchus behind it. The segmentary artery is divided using a stapler with a curved tip load. A vessel loop is used to retract the segmentary bronchus, facilitating the introduction of the stapler, which is then used to divide the bronchus with a vascular load featuring curved teeth.

The surgeon verifies the position of the anterior segment of the lung before applying the stapler to complete the division. After stapling, the lung expansion is carefully checked to ensure proper function.
